chunk_summary: This section discusses the use of skinfold thickness and body composition tools in anthropometric measurements, highlighting their applications, limitations, and practical considerations for growth monitoring in specific clinical contexts.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of skinfold thickness measurements in assessing body composition and growth velocity.
Identify clinical scenarios where advanced body composition tools like BIA and DXA are appropriate.
Recognize the limitations and challenges of using skinfold calipers and other adjunct tools in routine care.
Learn to document methods and devices used for anthropometric measurements to ensure valid follow-up comparisons.
key_facts: Skinfold thickness is measured at sites like triceps and subscapular using calibrated calipers, with readings taken 1 cm below the fingers after pressure stabilizes.
Serial skinfold measurements can indicate fat loss (wasting) or excess adiposity even when weight remains stable.
Bioelectrical impedance analysis (BIA) can detect sarcopenia or muscle wasting earlier than weight but is influenced by hydration status and device-specific equations.
Dual-energy x-ray absorptiometry (DXA) is the reference standard for body composition but is rarely justified solely for growth monitoring.
Skinfold measurements are most useful in obesity clinics, sports medicine, and chronic conditions like HIV, CKD, or severe CP where weight and height may not reflect true body composition.
Inter-observer variability in skinfold measurements is high without repeated training, and edema invalidates fat-store inference.
reference_quote: Skinfold thickness and body composition tools can add depth when weight or BMI alone is misleading. (Source: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/sites/books/NBK537315/)
